data.description: Background: EFSA's remit and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to “food and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues”. Risk assessment has been defined as "a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation" (EC, 2002).  In the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as “reference values1” to protect human health, animal health, environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using a “reference point2" determined from the critical toxicological study on which an uncertainty factor3 is applied. Since its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for more than 5,700 unique substances in over 2,400 Scientific Opinions, Statements and Conclusions through the work of its scientific Panels, Units and Scientific Committee.    EFSA's Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox OpenFoodTox is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed additives and pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA’s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value. The data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using OECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs) as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity. OpenFoodTox contributes actively to EFSA’s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of widening EFSA’s evidence base and optimising access to its data as a valuable open source toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD’s Global Portal to Information on Chemical Substances (eChemPortal) so that individual substances can be searched as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).   What's new? OpenFoodTox 2.0  The 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains physico-chemical properties and pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic data for more than 850 substances. In order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded: 1. Six individual spreadsheets including a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d) reference values, e) genotoxicity and f) phys-chem&toxicokinetics data*. 2. The full database*.   OpenFoodTox 2.0 and innovative in silico models In silico models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment (bees and rainbow trout) and human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b; Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These in silico models provide alternative means to animal experiments for the hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement) (Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).       Definitions 1 Reference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018). 2 Reference Point: defined point on an experimental dose–response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018). 3 Uncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009). *Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and OP_ID keys as available in "PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023" and "OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023".   This version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022

  <section id="description" class="rel-mt-2 rich-input-content" aria-label="Record description">
    <h2 id="description-heading" class="sr-only">Description</h2>
    
    <div style="word-wrap: break-word;">
      <p><strong>Background: EFSA's remit and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants</strong></p>
<p>The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to “food and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues”. Risk assessment has been defined as "a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation" (EC, 2002). </p>
<p>In the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as “reference values<sup>1</sup>” to protect human health, animal health, environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using a “reference point<sup>2</sup>" determined from the critical toxicological study on which an uncertainty factor<sup>3</sup> is applied.</p>
<p>Since its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for <strong>more than 5,700 unique substances</strong> in <strong>over 2,400 Scientific Opinions</strong><strong>, Statements and Conclusions</strong> through the work of its <a href="http://www.efsa.europa.eu/en/science/scientific-committee-and-panels">scientific Panels</a>, Units and Scientific Committee. </p>
<p> </p>
<p><strong>EFSA's Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox</strong></p>
<p>OpenFoodTox is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed additives and pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA’s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value.</p>
<p>The data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using <a href="https://www.oecd.org/ehs/templates/">OECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs)</a> as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity.</p>
<p>OpenFoodTox contributes actively to EFSA’s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of widening EFSA’s evidence base and optimising access to its data as a valuable open source toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD’s Global Portal to Information on Chemical Substances (<a href="https://www.echemportal.org/">eChemPortal</a>) so that individual substances can be searched as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).</p>
<p> </p>
<p><strong>What's new? OpenFoodTox 2.0 </strong></p>
<p>The 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains <strong>physico-chemical properties</strong> and <strong>pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic</strong> data for more than 850 substances.</p>
<p>In order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded:</p>
<p><strong>1. </strong>Six individual spreadsheets including a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d) reference values, e) genotoxicity and f) phys-chem&toxicokinetics data*.</p>
<p><strong>2. </strong>The full database*.</p>
<p> </p>
<p><strong>OpenFoodTox 2.0 and innovative <em>in silico</em> models</strong></p>
<p><em>In silico</em> models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment (bees and rainbow trout) and human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b; Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These <em>in silico </em>models provide alternative means to animal experiments for the hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement) (Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).</p>
<p> </p>
<p> </p>
<p> </p>
<p><strong>Definitions</strong></p>
<p><sup>1 </sup>Reference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018).</p>
<p><sup>2 </sup>Reference Point: defined point on an experimental dose–response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018).</p>
<p><sup>3 </sup>Uncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009).</p>
<p>*Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and OP_ID keys as available in "PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023" and "OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023".</p>
<p> </p>
<p><strong>This version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022</strong></p>
    </div>

"identifiers": [{"identifier": "0000-0003-3305-063X", "scheme": "orcid"}], "name": "Dorne, Jean Lou C.M.", "type": "personal"}}], "description": "\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eBackground: EFSA\u0027s remit\u0026nbsp;and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to \u0026ldquo;food and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues\u0026rdquo;. Risk assessment has been defined as \"a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation\"\u0026nbsp;(EC, 2002).\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIn the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as\u0026nbsp;\u0026ldquo;reference values\u003csup\u003e1\u003c/sup\u003e\u0026rdquo; to protect human health, animal health,\u0026nbsp;environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using\u0026nbsp;a\u0026nbsp;\u0026ldquo;reference point\u003csup\u003e2\u003c/sup\u003e\" determined from the critical toxicological study\u0026nbsp;on which an uncertainty factor\u003csup\u003e3\u003c/sup\u003e is applied.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSince its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for \u003cstrong\u003emore than 5,700 unique substances\u003c/strong\u003e in \u003cstrong\u003eover 2,400\u0026nbsp;Scientific Opinions\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e, Statements and Conclusions\u003c/strong\u003e through the work of its \u003ca href=\"http://www.efsa.europa.eu/en/science/scientific-committee-and-panels\"\u003escientific Panels\u003c/a\u003e, Units and Scientific Committee.\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eEFSA\u0027s Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eOpenFoodTox\u0026nbsp;is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed\u0026nbsp;additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed\u0026nbsp;additives and\u0026nbsp;pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA\u0026rsquo;s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using \u003ca href=\"https://www.oecd.org/ehs/templates/\"\u003eOECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs)\u003c/a\u003e\u0026nbsp;as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eOpenFoodTox\u0026nbsp;contributes actively to EFSA\u0026rsquo;s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of\u0026nbsp;widening EFSA\u0026rsquo;s evidence base and optimising\u0026nbsp;access to its data as a valuable open source\u0026nbsp;toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in\u0026nbsp;chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD\u0026rsquo;s Global Portal\u0026nbsp;to Information on Chemical Substances (\u003ca href=\"https://www.echemportal.org/\"\u003eeChemPortal\u003c/a\u003e) so that individual substances can be searched\u0026nbsp;as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eWhat\u0027s new? OpenFoodTox 2.0\u0026nbsp;\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains \u003cstrong\u003ephysico-chemical properties\u003c/strong\u003e and \u003cstrong\u003epharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic\u003c/strong\u003e data for more than 850 substances.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIn order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded:\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e1. \u003c/strong\u003eSix individual spreadsheets including\u0026nbsp;a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d)\u0026nbsp;reference values,\u0026nbsp;e)\u0026nbsp;genotoxicity and f) phys-chem\u0026amp;toxicokinetics data*.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e2. \u003c/strong\u003eThe full database*.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eOpenFoodTox 2.0 and\u0026nbsp;innovative\u0026nbsp;\u003cem\u003ein silico\u003c/em\u003e\u0026nbsp;models\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cem\u003eIn silico\u003c/em\u003e models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment\u0026nbsp;(bees and\u0026nbsp;rainbow trout) and\u0026nbsp;human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b;\u0026nbsp;Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These \u003cem\u003ein silico\u0026nbsp;\u003c/em\u003emodels provide alternative means to animal experiments for the\u0026nbsp;hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement)\u0026nbsp;(Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eDefinitions\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e1\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eReference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e2\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eReference Point: defined point on an experimental dose\u0026ndash;response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e3\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eUncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e*Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and\u0026nbsp;OP_ID keys as available in\u0026nbsp;\"PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023\" and \"OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023\".\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eThis version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e", "languages": [{"id": "eng", "title": {"en": "English"}}], "publication_date": "2023-09-13", "publisher": "Zenodo", "references": [{"reference": "EC, 2002. remit\u00a0and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants\n\nThe European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to \u201cfood and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues\u201d. Risk assessment has been defined as \"a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation\"\u00a0(EC, 2002).\u00a0\n\nIn the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as\u00a0\u201creference values1\u201d to protect human health, animal health,\u00a0environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using\u00a0a\u00a0\u201creference point2\" determined from the critical toxicological study\u00a0on which an uncertainty factor3 is applied.\n\nSince its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for more than 5,700 unique substances in over 2,400\u00a0Scientific Opinions, Statements and Conclusions through the work of its scientific Panels, Units and Scientific Committee.\u00a0\n\n\u00a0\n\nEFSA\u0027s Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox\n\nOpenFoodTox\u00a0is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed\u00a0additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed\u00a0additives and\u00a0pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA\u2019s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value.\n\nThe data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using OECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs)\u00a0as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity.\n\nOpenFoodTox\u00a0contributes actively to EFSA\u2019s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of\u00a0widening EFSA\u2019s evidence base and optimising\u00a0access to its data as a valuable open source\u00a0toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in\u00a0chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD\u2019s Global Portal\u00a0to Information on Chemical Substances (eChemPortal) so that individual substances can be searched\u00a0as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).\n\n\u00a0\n\nWhat\u0027s new? OpenFoodTox 2.0\u00a0\n\nThe 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains physico-chemical properties and pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic data for more than 850 substances.\n\nIn order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded:\n\n1. Six individual spreadsheets including\u00a0a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d)\u00a0reference values,\u00a0e)\u00a0genotoxicity and f) phys-chem\u0026toxicokinetics data*.\n\n2. The full database*.\n\n\u00a0\n\nOpenFoodTox 2.0 and\u00a0innovative\u00a0in silico\u00a0models\n\nIn silico models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment\u00a0(bees and\u00a0rainbow trout) and\u00a0human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b;\u00a0Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These in silico\u00a0models provide alternative means to animal experiments for the\u00a0hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement)\u00a0(Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).\n\n\u00a0\n\n\u00a0\n\n\u00a0\n\nDefinitions\n\n1\u00a0Reference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018).\n\n2\u00a0Reference Point: defined point on an experimental dose\u2013response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018).\n\n3\u00a0Uncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009).\n\n*Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and\u00a0OP_ID keys as available in\u00a0\"PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023\" and \"OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023\".\n\n\u00a0\n\nThis version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022", "identifiers": [{"identifier": "0000-0003-3305-063X", "scheme": "orcid"}], "name": "Dorne, Jean Lou C.M.", "type": "personal"}}], "description": "\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eBackground: EFSA\u0027s remit\u0026nbsp;and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to \u0026ldquo;food and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues\u0026rdquo;. Risk assessment has been defined as \"a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation\"\u0026nbsp;(EC, 2002).\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIn the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as\u0026nbsp;\u0026ldquo;reference values\u003csup\u003e1\u003c/sup\u003e\u0026rdquo; to protect human health, animal health,\u0026nbsp;environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using\u0026nbsp;a\u0026nbsp;\u0026ldquo;reference point\u003csup\u003e2\u003c/sup\u003e\" determined from the critical toxicological study\u0026nbsp;on which an uncertainty factor\u003csup\u003e3\u003c/sup\u003e is applied.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSince its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for \u003cstrong\u003emore than 5,700 unique substances\u003c/strong\u003e in \u003cstrong\u003eover 2,400\u0026nbsp;Scientific Opinions\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e, Statements and Conclusions\u003c/strong\u003e through the work of its \u003ca href=\"http://www.efsa.europa.eu/en/science/scientific-committee-and-panels\"\u003escientific Panels\u003c/a\u003e, Units and Scientific Committee.\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eEFSA\u0027s Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eOpenFoodTox\u0026nbsp;is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed\u0026nbsp;additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed\u0026nbsp;additives and\u0026nbsp;pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA\u0026rsquo;s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using \u003ca href=\"https://www.oecd.org/ehs/templates/\"\u003eOECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs)\u003c/a\u003e\u0026nbsp;as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eOpenFoodTox\u0026nbsp;contributes actively to EFSA\u0026rsquo;s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of\u0026nbsp;widening EFSA\u0026rsquo;s evidence base and optimising\u0026nbsp;access to its data as a valuable open source\u0026nbsp;toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in\u0026nbsp;chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD\u0026rsquo;s Global Portal\u0026nbsp;to Information on Chemical Substances (\u003ca href=\"https://www.echemportal.org/\"\u003eeChemPortal\u003c/a\u003e) so that individual substances can be searched\u0026nbsp;as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eWhat\u0027s new? OpenFoodTox 2.0\u0026nbsp;\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains \u003cstrong\u003ephysico-chemical properties\u003c/strong\u003e and \u003cstrong\u003epharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic\u003c/strong\u003e data for more than 850 substances.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIn order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded:\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e1. \u003c/strong\u003eSix individual spreadsheets including\u0026nbsp;a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d)\u0026nbsp;reference values,\u0026nbsp;e)\u0026nbsp;genotoxicity and f) phys-chem\u0026amp;toxicokinetics data*.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e2. \u003c/strong\u003eThe full database*.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eOpenFoodTox 2.0 and\u0026nbsp;innovative\u0026nbsp;\u003cem\u003ein silico\u003c/em\u003e\u0026nbsp;models\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cem\u003eIn silico\u003c/em\u003e models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment\u0026nbsp;(bees and\u0026nbsp;rainbow trout) and\u0026nbsp;human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b;\u0026nbsp;Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These \u003cem\u003ein silico\u0026nbsp;\u003c/em\u003emodels provide alternative means to animal experiments for the\u0026nbsp;hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement)\u0026nbsp;(Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eDefinitions\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e1\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eReference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e2\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eReference Point: defined point on an experimental dose\u0026ndash;response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e3\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eUncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e*Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and\u0026nbsp;OP_ID keys as available in\u0026nbsp;\"PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023\" and \"OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023\".\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eThis version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e", "languages": [{"id": "eng", "title": {"en": "English"}}], "publication_date": "2023-09-13", "publisher": "Zenodo", "references": [{"reference": "EC, 2002. remit\u00a0and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants\n\nThe European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to \u201cfood and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues\u201d. Risk assessment has been defined as \"a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation\"\u00a0(EC, 2002).\u00a0\n\nIn the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as\u00a0\u201creference values1\u201d to protect human health, animal health,\u00a0environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using\u00a0a\u00a0\u201creference point2\" determined from the critical toxicological study\u00a0on which an uncertainty factor3 is applied.\n\nSince its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for more than 5,700 unique substances in over 2,400\u00a0Scientific Opinions, Statements and Conclusions through the work of its scientific Panels, Units and Scientific Committee.\u00a0\n\n\u00a0\n\nEFSA\u0027s Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox\n\nOpenFoodTox\u00a0is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed\u00a0additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed\u00a0additives and\u00a0pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA\u2019s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value.\n\nThe data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using OECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs)\u00a0as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity.\n\nOpenFoodTox\u00a0contributes actively to EFSA\u2019s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of\u00a0widening EFSA\u2019s evidence base and optimising\u00a0access to its data as a valuable open source\u00a0toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in\u00a0chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD\u2019s Global Portal\u00a0to Information on Chemical Substances (eChemPortal) so that individual substances can be searched\u00a0as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).\n\n\u00a0\n\nWhat\u0027s new? OpenFoodTox 2.0\u00a0\n\nThe 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains physico-chemical properties and pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic data for more than 850 substances.\n\nIn order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded:\n\n1. Six individual spreadsheets including\u00a0a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d)\u00a0reference values,\u00a0e)\u00a0genotoxicity and f) phys-chem\u0026toxicokinetics data*.\n\n2. The full database*.\n\n\u00a0\n\nOpenFoodTox 2.0 and\u00a0innovative\u00a0in silico\u00a0models\n\nIn silico models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment\u00a0(bees and\u00a0rainbow trout) and\u00a0human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b;\u00a0Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These in silico\u00a0models provide alternative means to animal experiments for the\u00a0hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement)\u00a0(Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).\n\n\u00a0\n\n\u00a0\n\n\u00a0\n\nDefinitions\n\n1\u00a0Reference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018).\n\n2\u00a0Reference Point: defined point on an experimental dose\u2013response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018).\n\n3\u00a0Uncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009).\n\n*Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and\u00a0OP_ID keys as available in\u00a0\"PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023\" and \"OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023\".\n\n\u00a0\n\nThis version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022", "datePublished": "2023-09-13", "description": "\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eBackground: EFSA\u0027s remit\u0026nbsp;and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to \u0026ldquo;food and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues\u0026rdquo;. Risk assessment has been defined as \"a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation\"\u0026nbsp;(EC, 2002).\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIn the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as\u0026nbsp;\u0026ldquo;reference values\u003csup\u003e1\u003c/sup\u003e\u0026rdquo; to protect human health, animal health,\u0026nbsp;environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using\u0026nbsp;a\u0026nbsp;\u0026ldquo;reference point\u003csup\u003e2\u003c/sup\u003e\" determined from the critical toxicological study\u0026nbsp;on which an uncertainty factor\u003csup\u003e3\u003c/sup\u003e is applied.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSince its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for \u003cstrong\u003emore than 5,700 unique substances\u003c/strong\u003e in \u003cstrong\u003eover 2,400\u0026nbsp;Scientific Opinions\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e, Statements and Conclusions\u003c/strong\u003e through the work of its \u003ca href=\"http://www.efsa.europa.eu/en/science/scientific-committee-and-panels\"\u003escientific Panels\u003c/a\u003e, Units and Scientific Committee.\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eEFSA\u0027s Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eOpenFoodTox\u0026nbsp;is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed\u0026nbsp;additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed\u0026nbsp;additives and\u0026nbsp;pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA\u0026rsquo;s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using \u003ca href=\"https://www.oecd.org/ehs/templates/\"\u003eOECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs)\u003c/a\u003e\u0026nbsp;as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eOpenFoodTox\u0026nbsp;contributes actively to EFSA\u0026rsquo;s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of\u0026nbsp;widening EFSA\u0026rsquo;s evidence base and optimising\u0026nbsp;access to its data as a valuable open source\u0026nbsp;toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in\u0026nbsp;chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD\u0026rsquo;s Global Portal\u0026nbsp;to Information on Chemical Substances (\u003ca href=\"https://www.echemportal.org/\"\u003eeChemPortal\u003c/a\u003e) so that individual substances can be searched\u0026nbsp;as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eWhat\u0027s new? OpenFoodTox 2.0\u0026nbsp;\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains \u003cstrong\u003ephysico-chemical properties\u003c/strong\u003e and \u003cstrong\u003epharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic\u003c/strong\u003e data for more than 850 substances.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIn order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded:\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e1. \u003c/strong\u003eSix individual spreadsheets including\u0026nbsp;a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d)\u0026nbsp;reference values,\u0026nbsp;e)\u0026nbsp;genotoxicity and f) phys-chem\u0026amp;toxicokinetics data*.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e2. \u003c/strong\u003eThe full database*.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eOpenFoodTox 2.0 and\u0026nbsp;innovative\u0026nbsp;\u003cem\u003ein silico\u003c/em\u003e\u0026nbsp;models\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cem\u003eIn silico\u003c/em\u003e models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment\u0026nbsp;(bees and\u0026nbsp;rainbow trout) and\u0026nbsp;human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b;\u0026nbsp;Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These \u003cem\u003ein silico\u0026nbsp;\u003c/em\u003emodels provide alternative means to animal experiments for the\u0026nbsp;hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement)\u0026nbsp;(Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eDefinitions\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e1\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eReference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e2\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eReference Point: defined point on an experimental dose\u0026ndash;response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003csup\u003e3\u0026nbsp;\u003c/sup\u003eUncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009).\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e*Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and\u0026nbsp;OP_ID keys as available in\u0026nbsp;\"PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023\" and \"OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023\".\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u0026nbsp;\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eThis version replaces version 5 metadata.description: Background: EFSA's remit and chemical risk assessment of regulated products and contaminants The European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) has the remit to provide scientific advice to risk managers and decision makers through risk assessment and risk communication on issues related to “food and feed safety, animal health and welfare, plant health, nutrition, and environmental issues”. Risk assessment has been defined as "a scientifically based process consisting of four steps: hazard identification, hazard characterisation, exposure assessment and risk characterisation" (EC, 2002).  In the food and feed safety area, hazard identification and hazard characterisation aim to determine safe levels of exposure for regulated products or contaminants as “reference values1” to protect human health, animal health, environmental-relevant species or the whole ecosystem. Such reference values for a given species are most often derived by using a “reference point2" determined from the critical toxicological study on which an uncertainty factor3 is applied. Since its creation in 2002, the European Food safety Authority (EFSA) has produced risk assessments for more than 5,700 unique substances in over 2,400 Scientific Opinions, Statements and Conclusions through the work of its scientific Panels, Units and Scientific Committee.    EFSA's Chemical Hazards Database: OpenFoodTox OpenFoodTox is a structured database summarising the outcomes of hazard identification and characterisation for the human health (all regulated products and contaminants), the animal health (feed additives, pesticides and contaminants) and the environment (feed additives and pesticides). It provides open-source data for the substance characterisation, the links to EFSA’s related outputs, background European legislation, and a summary of the critical toxicological endpoints and reference value. The data model of OpenFoodTox has been designed using OECD Harmonised Templates (OHTs) as a basis to collect and structure the data in a harmonised manner. OpenFoodTox provides open source data for the substance characterisation, EFSA outputs, reference points, reference values and genotoxicity. OpenFoodTox contributes actively to EFSA’s 2020 Science Strategy (EFSA, 2016) and to the aim of widening EFSA’s evidence base and optimising access to its data as a valuable open source toxicological database that can be shared with all scientific advisory bodies and stakeholders with an interest in chemical risk assessment. In addition, OpenFoodTox 2.0 has been submitted to the OECD’s Global Portal to Information on Chemical Substances (eChemPortal) so that individual substances can be searched as part of the national and international databases. Further description and associated references are described in the EFSA journal editorial (Dorne et al., 2017) and other publications (Dorne et al., 2020).   What's new? OpenFoodTox 2.0  The 2023 upgrade to OpenFoodTox 2.0 now contains physico-chemical properties and pharmacokinetic/toxicokinetic data for more than 850 substances. In order to disseminate OpenFoodTox 2.0 to a wider community, two sets of data can be downloaded: 1. Six individual spreadsheets including a) substance characterisation , b) EFSA outputs, c) reference points, d) reference values, e) genotoxicity and f) phys-chem&toxicokinetics data*. 2. The full database*.   OpenFoodTox 2.0 and innovative in silico models In silico models using OpenFoodTox 2.0 data have been developed for ecological risk assessment (bees and rainbow trout) and human risk assessment using rat toxicological data (Carnesecchi et al., 2020a; Carnesecchi et al., 2020b; Como et al., 2017; Benefenati et al., 2017; Toporov et al., 2017, Toporov et al., 2018). These in silico models provide alternative means to animal experiments for the hazard identification and characterisation of chemicals, and are becoming of increasing interest in the risk assessment community to deliver the 3Rs (replacement, reduction, refinement) (Hartung, 2004; OECD, 2005), particularly since the banning of animal testing for the approval of cosmetics as consumer products (Regulation (EC) No. 1223/2009, Art.18(2)).       Definitions 1 Reference Value: the estimated maximum dose (on a body mass basis) or the concentration of an agent to which an individual may be exposed over a specified period without appreciable risk. Reference values are established by applying an uncertainty factor to the reference point. Examples of reference values in human health include acceptable daily intake (ADI) for food and feed additives, and pesticides, tolerable upper intake levels (UL) for vitamins and minerals, and tolerable daily intake (TDI) for contaminants and food contact materials. For acute effects and operators, the acute reference dose (ARfD) and the acceptable operator exposure level (AOEL). In animal health and the ecological area, these include safe feed concentrations and the Predicted no effect concentration (PNEC) respectively (EFSA Scientific Committee, 2018). 2 Reference Point: defined point on an experimental dose–response relationship for the critical effect. This term is synonymous to Point of departure (USA). Reference points include the lowest or no observed adverse effect level (LOAEL/NOAEL) or benchmark dose lower confidence limit (BDML), used to derive a reference value or Margin of Exposure in human and animal health risk assessment. In the ecological area, these include lethal dose (LD50), effect concentration (EC5/ECx), no (adverse) effect concentration/dose (NOEC/NOAEC/NOAED), no (adverse) effect level (NEL/NOAEL), hazard concentration (HC5/HCx) derived from a Species Sensitivity Distributions (SSD) for the ecosystem (EFSA Scientific Committee,2018). 3 Uncertainty factor: reductive factor by which an observed or estimated no observed adverse effect level or other reference point, such as the benchmark dose or benchmark dose lower confidence limit, is divided to arrive at a reference dose or standard that is considered safe or without appreciable risk (WHO, 2009). *Phys-chem and toxicokinetics data can be merged with the full database using SUB_COM_ID and OP_ID keys as available in "PhysChem_Toxicokinetics_KJ_2023" and "OpenFoodToxTX22809_2023".   This version replaces version 5 to include EFSA Opinions, Statements and Conclusions up to Sept 2022
